moment_text: I was not getting the answers that I needed from doctors, and I was getting very frustrated. moment_text: Yeah, I mean, I started the menopause transition, uh, probably around age 47 or 48. It took a while before I realized what was happening, and, and that's strange because I have a biology degree. I was a health inspector, like I should have known. More about what was happening to me and it just wasn't adding up. It was this collection of symptoms. It didn't make sense. I didn't know if it was heart issues, if it was, you know, mental issues if I didn't know what issues. But it was a lot of issues. And um, it kind of started with cardiac issues that I couldn't get answers on and progressed. At one point, I had a moment of clarity where I thought. Oh, this is hot flashes, you know? 'cause I would sit down and I was always very conscientious of my appearance and I would sit down and do my makeup and do my hair, and then all of a sudden I'd have this big sweat and then my hair would just fall flat. And it was so distressing to me, like, why is this happening? And then sweating all night and being, having my sleep interrupted and I'm not really sleeping. Well, for probably three solid years, which really got me to that point where I was just not able to concentrate, irritable, couldn't relate to people.

What you think their goals are, what you think they're trying to get out of that. And that's really important in the business of, you know, passing legislation. You know, like you put in a bill and you end up having to cut three things out of six of the bill, but you're making incremental progress. It doesn't preclude you from maybe later going back and trying to make these other changes. That's what the next session, there's always another session.
